Core Meaning: An earned value convention that credits zero percent of an activity until it is finished, then 100 percent on completion.
Expansion: The 0/100 rule is a conservative earned value technique for assigning credit to work in progress, recognizing value only when an activity is fully complete. It avoids subjective in-progress estimates and is best suited to short activities that start and finish within a single reporting period. Applying it to long activities understates progress, so it is matched to small work packages. It is one of several fixed-formula rules used to measure earned value.
Contrast: 50/50 rule — the 50/50 rule credits half the value at start, whereas the 0/100 rule credits nothing until completion.
Example: A two-day inspection task earns no value until it is signed off, then books its full budgeted value.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: An earned value convention that credits 50 percent of an activity when it starts and the remaining 50 percent when it completes.
Expansion: The 50/50 rule is a fixed-formula earned value technique that recognizes partial progress without requiring subjective percent-complete estimates. Booking half the value at start gives early credit for work under way, while withholding the rest until completion guards against overstating progress. It suits activities spanning about two reporting periods. Other split rules such as 25/75 follow the same logic with different weightings.
Contrast: 0/100 rule — the 0/100 rule gives no credit until completion, whereas the 50/50 rule recognizes half the value as soon as work begins.
Example: On starting the data-migration task, the team books 50 percent of its budget and the rest only when migration is verified.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: The realised cost incurred for the work performed on an activity during a specific time period.
Expansion: Actual Cost is the amount actually spent for work completed in a given period. It is a core earned value concept used to compare planned spending with real spending and assess cost performance. Actual Cost may be tracked at the activity, work package, or control account level depending on how the project is controlled.
Contrast: Planned Value — Actual Cost is what was actually spent; Planned Value is the authorised budget assigned to scheduled work.
Example: A testing work package was budgeted at 10,000 for the month, but labour and tool charges totalled 12,000, so the Actual Cost for that period is 12,000.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Actual Cost

Core Meaning: An activity whose effort is directly tied to and distributed in proportion to another discrete work effort.
Expansion: Apportioned effort is support work that is planned as a proportion of related discrete effort rather than estimated independently. Its duration and resource use depend on the work it accompanies, such as quality assurance tied to production effort. PMI uses the term to distinguish this type of work from both discrete effort and level of effort in scheduling and earned value contexts.
Contrast: Level of effort — apportioned effort varies in proportion to related discrete work; level of effort is ongoing support work not measured by discrete output.
Example: A testing oversight task is budgeted at a fixed percentage of the application-development effort and scales up or down as development work changes.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), apportioned effort, page 700

Core Meaning: A credible estimate treated as dependable because it is grounded in validated data and expert judgement.
Expansion: PMI does not define authoritative estimate as a formal glossary term. In practice, project teams use it to describe an estimate that carries strong confidence because its basis, assumptions, and review are robust.
Contrast: Basis of estimate — authoritative estimate describes the credibility of the estimate, while a basis of estimate explains the assumptions, methods, and sources behind it.
Example: Before bid submission, the project manager uses reviewed supplier quotes, labour productivity history, and scope assumptions to produce an estimate the steering team treats as authoritative.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The documented rationale that explains how an estimate was developed, including methods, assumptions, constraints, source data, and confidence considerations.
Expansion: Basis of estimate records the reasoning behind a cost, duration, resource, or effort estimate so that others can understand what the estimate means and how reliable it is. It normally identifies the estimating approach used, the scope covered, assumptions made, exclusions, constraints, reference data, and any range or confidence limits. PMI treats it as essential because estimates are decisions as much as numbers: without their basis, they cannot be reviewed, challenged, updated, or governed effectively. It is especially important when estimates are approved, baselined, or handed off across teams and phases.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Basis of estimate is the supporting explanation attached to an estimate, not the estimate itself.

It captures the elements used to arrive at the figure:

- The logic.
- The inputs.
- The assumptions.
- The exclusions.
- The methods.

Why it matters and where it applies: estimates influence funding, commitments, scheduling, procurement, and stakeholder expectations. A clear basis of estimate improves transparency and makes it easier to assess confidence, compare options, and update forecasts as conditions change.

The most common misconception: that an estimate alone is enough if the number looks reasonable. In practice, a number without context is difficult to validate and often becomes misleading when scope, rates, productivity assumptions, or constraints are later questioned.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team attaches notes to a cost estimate showing the analogous and vendor-based inputs used, the assumptions about team capacity, the exclusions for training and support, and the contingency logic reviewed during approval.
Contrast: Estimate — the estimate is the resulting value or range, while the basis of estimate explains how that value or range was derived.
Example: A project manager presents a release cost estimate together with the labour-rate assumptions, supplier quotes, productivity factors, exclusions, and confidence range used to build it.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Basis of estimates, index entry on page 116

Core Meaning: A measure that compares the expected benefits of an initiative to its expected costs to support selection and justification decisions.
Expansion: Benefit-cost ratio expresses the relationship between estimated benefits and estimated costs as an economic decision metric. In project and program contexts, it helps compare alternatives and assess whether an investment is attractive enough to pursue. A higher ratio generally indicates a more favourable business case, but it should be interpreted alongside assumptions, risk, timing, and strategic fit.
Contrast: Net present value — benefit-cost ratio compares relative benefits to costs, while net present value expresses absolute value after discounting cash flows.
Example: A portfolio review board compares two automation projects and prefers the one with a stronger benefit-cost ratio because it delivers more forecast value per unit of investment.
Source: PMI Lexicon — confirm exact citation in PMI source

Core Meaning: The approved estimate for the project, program, portfolio, or a component of that work.
Expansion: A budget is the authorised amount of money allocated to perform defined work. PMI uses the term as the approved estimate, which means it is more than an early calculation and becomes part of the basis for monitoring and control. Budgets may exist at multiple levels, from the total project to control accounts, work packages, or activities, depending on how the work is managed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A budget is the approved financial expression of planned work. It converts estimates into an authorised spending framework against which actual cost performance can be tracked.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects need a clear spending boundary to support funding decisions, control, and reporting. Budgeting is central in cost management and is used across predictive, hybrid, and governed adaptive environments whenever financial accountability matters.

The most common misconception: that a budget is just an estimate. An estimate predicts likely cost, while a budget is the approved amount used for authorisation and control.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager rolls up labour, vendor, equipment, and contingency amounts into an approved project budget, then compares monthly actuals against that approved figure to identify variances and escalation needs.
Contrast: Estimate — an estimate forecasts probable cost; a budget is the approved amount authorised for the work.
Example: A data-center migration project receives an approved budget covering hardware, consulting support, testing, and cutover activities, and the team tracks actual spending against it each month.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, budget

Core Meaning: The sum of all approved budgets established for the work to be performed.
Expansion: Budget at Completion, or BAC, is the total planned value of the project or control account when all authorised work is included. It is a key earned value management reference point because it represents the full approved budget for the scope. Teams use it with cost and schedule performance data to forecast likely final cost and assess performance trends.
Contrast: Estimate at Completion — Budget at Completion is the approved total planned budget; Estimate at Completion is the current forecast of what the total cost will actually be.
Example: On an infrastructure upgrade, the team sets the BAC after finalising the cost baseline, then compares it with the running Estimate at Completion as performance data accumulates.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, budget at completion

Core Meaning: An estimate developed to establish or refine an approved budget for a project or component of work.
Expansion: A budgetary estimate is prepared early enough to support funding and cost planning decisions, but with less precision than later detailed estimates. In PMI usage, it helps set expectations for the likely cost range of the work and supports approval, prioritisation, or portfolio decisions. Its accuracy depends on the amount of scope definition, assumptions, and historical information available at the time.
Contrast: Definitive estimate — a budgetary estimate supports budgeting with a broader expected range, while a definitive estimate is developed later from more complete scope detail for tighter control.
Example: Before approving a warehouse-automation project, the sponsor uses a budgetary estimate based on conceptual design, benchmark data, and major cost assumptions to set an initial funding target.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, section 7.2 Estimate Costs, page 245

Core Meaning: The movement of money into and out of a project, program, or organisation over time.
Expansion: Cash flow reflects when funds are received and when payments are made, not just the total budget or cost. In project management, it is used to understand funding needs, payment timing, and financial viability across the life of the work. Monitoring cash flow helps planners align expenditures, contractual payments, and resource commitments so that the project remains financially supportable.
Contrast: Cost baseline — cash flow tracks the timing of actual or planned money movement, while the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used to measure cost performance.
Example: An infrastructure project schedules major equipment payments in the second and third quarters and uses the cash-flow forecast to ensure funding is available before supplier invoices come due.
Source: PMI Lexicon — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: A numbering or coding system used to monitor project costs by category.
Expansion: The chart of accounts structures cost information into defined categories so expenditures can be collected, tracked, and reported consistently. It supports budgeting, accounting, and cost control by aligning project financial data with the organization's accounting framework. In project practice, it helps teams see where money is being spent and compare actual costs with planned allocations.
Contrast: Code of accounts — a chart of accounts organizes financial cost categories, while a code of accounts is used to identify elements in the work breakdown structure.
Example: A facilities project tracks spending under separate account categories for internal labor, purchased materials, leased equipment, and subcontracted services.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Glossary — Chart of accounts

Core Meaning: A management control point where scope, budget, actual cost, and schedule are integrated and compared to earned value for performance measurement.
Expansion: A control account sits above one or more work packages in the WBS and provides a practical point for planning and monitoring performance. It is where authorised scope, budgets, schedules, and actuals come together so the team can track progress and variance in a manageable way. It is especially important in earned value management because it creates a consistent unit for integrated control.
Contrast: Work package — a work package is the lowest level of planned work in the WBS; a control account is a higher-level management point used to integrate and measure several work elements.
Example: In an aircraft upgrade project, Avionics Integration is set up as a control account containing several work packages for design, installation, and testing, each rolled up for performance reporting.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring the status of the project to update the project costs and manage changes to the cost baseline.
Expansion: Control costs tracks actual spending against the approved cost baseline and identifies variances that may require corrective action or reforecasting. It uses performance information to determine whether the project remains within authorised funding levels. The process also supports forecasting and formal budget change control when adjustments are needed.
Contrast: Determine budget — Determine budget establishes the approved cost baseline; Control costs monitors actual cost performance against that baseline.
Example: A cloud migration project reviews actual vendor invoices against the budget and revises the forecast when infrastructure costs trend above plan.
Source: PMI Lexicon — control costs

Core Meaning: Summing work package cost estimates up through control accounts to build the total project cost.
Expansion: Cost aggregation is a technique in the Determine Budget process. Individual activity and work package estimates are rolled up into control accounts, and the control accounts are then rolled up into the overall project cost. This bottom-up summation produces the figures that feed the cost baseline.
Contrast: Cost estimating - estimating produces the cost of individual activities or packages, whereas aggregation rolls those estimates up into control accounts and the project total.
Example: A PM totals the costs of all work packages under each control account, then sums the control accounts to establish the project budget.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Determine Budget (cost aggregation)

Core Meaning: The approved version of the time-phased project budget, excluding any management reserves, that is used as a basis for comparison to actual results.
Expansion: The cost baseline is the authorised budget framework against which project cost performance is measured. It distributes planned costs over time so the team can compare what was supposed to be spent with what was actually spent. PMI treats it as a controlled baseline that may be changed only through formal change control.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The cost baseline is the approved, time-phased version of the project budget used for performance measurement. It provides the financial reference point for tracking actual costs, variances, and trends over the life of the project.

Why it matters and where it applies: without a stable cost baseline, the team cannot meaningfully control spending or evaluate cost performance. It is central to cost control, earned value analysis, forecasting, and financial reporting.

The most common misconception: that the cost baseline is identical to the total project budget. The distinction matters because the total funding picture can include management reserve, while the cost baseline used for performance measurement excludes it.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager compares monthly actual expenditures and earned value results against the approved phased baseline, then raises formal changes if the authorised spending plan itself must be revised.
Contrast: Project budget — the cost baseline is the approved time-phased basis for measuring cost performance; the broader project budget can include additional reserve amounts beyond the baseline.
Example: An ERP implementation approves a phased cost baseline for design, build, testing, and deployment, then tracks actual spend against each phase over time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 699

Core Meaning: The process of aggregating the estimated costs of individual activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline.
Expansion: Cost budgeting turns detailed estimates into an approved project budget structure. It aligns planned spending with scope, schedule, reserves, and control accounts so performance can be measured over time. In practice, it connects estimating with baseline approval and funding visibility.
Contrast: Cost estimate — a cost estimate predicts the likely cost of work; cost budgeting aggregates approved estimates into the authorized baseline for control.
Example: After estimating site preparation, equipment installation, testing, and training, a construction team rolls the figures up into a phased budget for executive approval.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms, Version 4.0, page 7

Core Meaning: The process of monitoring project cost performance and managing changes to the cost baseline.
Expansion: Cost control is the project management work of tracking actual spending, comparing results against the approved cost baseline, and taking action when variances or trends emerge. It includes influencing factors that create cost changes, ensuring approved changes are reflected in plans, and keeping stakeholders informed about performance. In PMI practice, it is central to maintaining financial discipline throughout execution and closing.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost control is the ongoing management process used to measure, understand, and respond to project cost performance. It combines performance data, forecasting, change control, and corrective action to keep spending aligned with approved expectations.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects rarely unfold exactly as planned, so costs shift as scope, risks, resource rates, and productivity change. Effective cost control helps managers detect issues early, protect funding, and support informed trade-off decisions across schedule, scope, quality, and risk.

The most common misconception: that cost control means simply cutting spending. In practice, it is about disciplined visibility and decision making, which may lead to reducing costs, increasing investment in justified areas, or formally updating baselines.

How it shows up in real projects: teams compare earned value data to the cost baseline, investigate cost variances, approve or reject cost-related change requests, and revise forecasts such as estimate at completion.
Contrast: Cost estimating — cost estimating predicts likely costs before or during planning; cost control monitors and manages cost performance against the approved baseline.
Example: On a construction project, the project manager detects a negative cost variance in structural steel work, investigates supplier rate changes, and raises a change request to address the forecast overrun.
Source: PMBOK Guide / not page-confirmed from available source text

Core Meaning: An approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: A cost estimate predicts how much money will be needed for activities, work packages, or the full project. It is developed using available information, assumptions, and estimating methods appropriate to the level of detail known at the time. Cost estimates support budgeting, funding decisions, and cost control. They may be refined as the project progresses and more information becomes available.
Contrast: Cost baseline — A cost estimate predicts likely cost; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for control.
Example: Before procurement begins, a project team estimates the cost of hardware, installation, and testing for a new data platform.
Source: Practice Standard for Project Estimating — Cost estimate

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Cost estimating quantifies the likely cost of activities, work packages, and the overall project to support budgeting and decisions. Estimators draw on techniques such as analogous, parametric, and bottom-up estimating, and they express results with a stated accuracy range. Estimates are refined as the project progresses from rough order of magnitude toward definitive figures.
Contrast: Cost budgeting — cost estimating produces the expected cost of individual elements, while budgeting aggregates those estimates into an authorized cost baseline.
Example: Using parametric rates per square metre, a quantity surveyor estimates the cost of a new office fit-out before the budget is set.
Source: PMBOK Guide — Estimate Costs

Core Meaning: Predictions of a project's future cost performance, typically expressed as EAC, ETC, and TCPI.
Expansion: Cost forecasts project where a project's costs are heading based on performance data available at the time. They are usually expressed through earned value measures such as estimate at completion (EAC), estimate to complete (ETC), budget at completion (BAC), and the to-complete performance index (TCPI). They give stakeholders an early, data-based warning of overruns or underruns so they can act.
Contrast: Cost baseline - the baseline is the approved planned spend, whereas a cost forecast projects where actual costs are now trending.
Example: Midway through the project, the PM calculates an EAC showing the work will finish about 10 percent over budget at the current spend rate.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Control Costs (work performance information)

Core Meaning: A knowledge area or discipline concerned with planning, estimating, budgeting, financing, funding, managing, and controlling costs so the project can be completed within the approved budget.
Expansion: Cost management covers the full set of activities used to define, organize, authorize, and control project spending. It begins with how costs will be managed, continues through estimating and budgeting, and extends into performance monitoring during delivery. PMI frames it as essential to financial predictability and informed decision-making across the project life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost management is the discipline that governs how a project plans and controls money. It includes the methods, baselines, thresholds, and reporting practices that keep financial performance visible and actionable.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project operates under resource constraints, and weak cost management can undermine scope, schedule, quality, and stakeholder confidence. It applies in predictive, adaptive, and hybrid settings, though the techniques and level of detail may differ.

The most common misconception: that cost management is only accounting or budget tracking after work starts. In practice it begins early with estimation logic, reserve decisions, and the design of a realistic baseline against which performance can be measured.

How it shows up in real projects: teams prepare estimates, establish a phased budget, define variance thresholds, review earned value or other performance signals, and take corrective action when spending trends threaten approved limits.
Contrast: Financial management — cost management focuses on the project-level planning and control of spending; financial management is broader and covers organizational funding, accounting, and financial governance.
Example: A product-development project defines estimating methods, sets a cost baseline by release, tracks monthly variances, and uses reserve analysis when supplier prices rise.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Sixth Edition, page 235

Core Meaning: A component of the project or program management plan that describes how costs will be planned, structured, and controlled.
Expansion: The cost management plan sets the rules for estimating, budgeting, managing, and controlling project costs. It defines matters such as units of measure, levels of precision, control thresholds, and reporting formats. It gives the team a consistent basis for cost decisions and performance monitoring.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost management plan defines how cost work will be performed and governed; the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for measurement.
Example: On a factory-upgrade project, the team documents that estimates will be produced in labor hours and local currency, tracked monthly, and escalated if variance exceeds the defined threshold.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition, page 241

Core Meaning: The amount by which actual project costs exceed the budgeted or baselined cost.
Expansion: A cost overrun occurs when the money actually spent surpasses the cost baseline, signalling unfavorable cost performance — a negative cost variance and a CPI below 1.0. Overruns may stem from poor estimating, scope creep, realized risks, or inefficiency, and are detected through earned value analysis and variance monitoring. The response is to analyze the cause and apply corrective or preventive action through the change-control and governance processes.
Contrast: Sunk cost — a cost overrun is spend above plan; a sunk cost is money already spent that should not influence future decisions.
Example: Halfway through, $600k is spent against a $500k earned-value baseline, a $100k cost overrun with CPI below 1.0.
Source: Common practitioner term; relates to cost variance and CPI in the PMBOK Guide Eighth Edition.

Core Meaning: A measure of the cost efficiency of budgeted resources, expressed as the ratio of earned value to actual cost.
Expansion: Cost Performance Index, or CPI, shows how efficiently the project is using cost relative to the value of work completed. It is one of the central earned value metrics for monitoring cost performance and forecasting future financial outcomes. A CPI greater than 1.0 indicates better cost efficiency than planned, while a value below 1.0 indicates poorer cost efficiency.
Contrast: Cost variance — Cost Performance Index is a ratio of efficiency; cost variance is the numerical difference between earned value and actual cost.
Example: A project that has earned 90 units of value while spending 100 units of cost has a CPI of 0.9, signaling cost inefficiency.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, Sixth Edition, Glossary, page 700

Core Meaning: A technique used to assess how uncertainty may affect project cost outcomes and overall budget exposure.
Expansion: Cost risk analysis examines the likelihood and impact of cost-related uncertainties on a project or programme. It is commonly used to estimate contingency needs, test the range of possible cost outcomes, and improve confidence in funding decisions. In PMI usage, it is treated as a risk-analysis practice rather than a formal core glossary term.
Contrast: Quantitative risk analysis — cost risk analysis focuses specifically on cost uncertainty, while quantitative risk analysis may address broader project objectives such as schedule, scope, or overall performance.
Example: Before approving a construction budget, the team models the possible effect of material-price volatility and productivity variation to estimate the probability of exceeding the approved cost baseline.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The amount of budget deficit or surplus at a given point in time, expressed as the difference between earned value and actual cost.
Expansion: Cost variance shows whether the project is spending more or less than the value of work completed. A positive variance indicates favorable cost performance, while a negative variance indicates an unfavorable cost condition. It is one of the primary earned value metrics used to monitor cost control. Because it is a point-in-time measure, it should be interpreted alongside trends and forecasts.
Contrast: Cost Performance Index — Cost variance is the numerical difference between earned value and actual cost; Cost Performance Index is the ratio of earned value to actual cost.
Example: If a project has earned value of 120 and actual cost of 100, the cost variance is 20, showing favorable cost performance.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Cost variance

Core Meaning: An analysis technique that compares expected project costs with anticipated benefits to support selection and justification decisions.
Expansion: Cost benefit analysis is used to determine whether the expected value of a proposed project or change justifies the resources required to deliver it. In PMI practice, it commonly appears in business-case thinking, project selection, and evaluation of response options by comparing financial and nonfinancial benefits against implementation and operating costs. It supports decision making but does not replace strategic judgment, risk consideration, or benefit realisation planning.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Cost benefit analysis is a structured comparison of what an initiative is expected to cost and what value it is expected to produce. The value side may include revenue, savings, risk reduction, compliance, service improvement, or strategic benefit.

Why it matters and where it applies: organisations use it when selecting projects, evaluating alternatives, approving changes, or assessing response strategies. It helps decision makers allocate limited resources to work that is expected to produce the greatest overall benefit.

The most common misconception: that it is only a finance exercise based on immediate cash return. In practice, project teams often include qualitative and longer-term benefits as well, while still recognising uncertainty and assumptions.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor compares several automation options, estimates implementation cost and expected operational savings, and uses the analysis to justify which option moves forward.
Contrast: Benefit-cost ratio — cost benefit analysis is the broader evaluation process; a benefit-cost ratio is one metric that may be produced from that analysis.
Example: A PMO evaluates whether replacing a manual reporting process with a dashboard platform is justified by reduced labour time, fewer reporting errors, and faster decision making.
Source: PMBOK Guide / not page-confirmed from available source text

Core Meaning: The total cost accrued over time up to a specific point in the project.
Expansion: Cumulative cost shows the running total of actual, planned, or forecast cost as the project progresses. It is commonly displayed on cost curves and earned value charts to show spending trends over time.
Contrast: Periodic cost — cumulative cost is the running total to date, while periodic cost is the amount incurred during a single reporting period.
Example: By the end of month four, the dashboard shows a cumulative cost of all labour, materials, and subcontractor charges incurred since project start.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The most accurate type of estimate, prepared from detailed information and used when project scope and activities are well understood.
Expansion: A definitive estimate is developed after substantial planning detail is available and is therefore expected to be more reliable than rough order of magnitude or budgetary estimates. PMI associates it with later planning stages when work packages, resource needs, and assumptions are defined with greater precision. It is commonly used to establish the performance baseline and support final approval decisions.
Contrast: Budgetary estimate — a definitive estimate is based on much more detailed and mature information and is therefore intended to be more accurate.
Example: After finalising the engineering design and procurement packages, the project team prepares a definitive cost estimate to establish the approved cost baseline.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Definitive estimate

Core Meaning: The accounting allocation of an asset's cost over its useful life to reflect its declining value.
Expansion: Depreciation spreads the cost of a tangible asset across the periods in which it provides benefit, recognizing that the asset loses value through use and age. It affects how project capital expenditures appear in financial statements and influences cost and benefit analyses used to justify projects. Methods include straight-line depreciation and accelerated approaches that recognize more expense in early years.
Contrast: Straight-line depreciation spreads cost evenly, while accelerated methods recognize larger amounts in the asset's early life.
Example: A company depreciates a new machine over ten years, expensing a portion of its cost each year.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: An estimate developed with a high level of detail, usually by estimating individual components of work and rolling them up.
Expansion: A detailed estimate is built from the bottom up using specific work elements rather than broad analogies or rough judgment. It is typically more accurate than high-level estimating because it considers the individual parts of the work, their quantities, and their resource needs. PMI uses this approach when enough information exists to support component-level estimating and validation.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — a detailed estimate is built from specific components; analogous estimating relies on comparison with similar past work.
Example: For a data center move, the team estimates cabling, rack installation, testing, and cutover separately, then rolls the amounts into one project estimate.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Detailed estimate, page 716

Core Meaning: The process of aggregating the estimated costs of individual activities or work packages to establish an authorized cost baseline.
Expansion: Determine Budget combines approved cost estimates into a time-phased funding baseline for the project. It establishes the cost baseline used for monitoring and controlling expenditures and performance. The process also considers reserves so uncertainty is reflected in a disciplined way rather than handled informally.
Contrast: Estimate Costs — Determine Budget aggregates approved estimates into the project cost baseline; Estimate Costs develops the individual cost estimates first.
Example: A product launch team rolls up design, testing, training, deployment, and contingency amounts into the approved project budget.
Source: PMBOK Guide glossary, Determine Budget, page 715

Core Meaning: Costs that can be traced directly and solely to a specific project's work.
Expansion: Direct costs are expenses incurred specifically for one project, so they can be attributed directly to its activities. Typical examples are the salaries of dedicated team members, project-specific materials, and equipment used only on that project. They are distinguished from indirect costs (shared overhead) and are central to building an accurate, defensible project budget.
Contrast: Indirect costs - direct costs are traceable to one project, whereas indirect costs such as overhead and general administration are shared across many projects.
Example: The wages of a contractor hired only for this project and the steel purchased exclusively for it are direct costs.
Source: PMBOK Guide - cost classification (direct vs indirect costs)

Core Meaning: The discount rate is the rate used to convert future cash flows into present value for financial evaluation and project selection.
Expansion: Within PMI project management practice, the discount rate is used in investment appraisal techniques such as net present value to account for the time value of money and risk. It helps organizations compare initiatives with different timing of costs and benefits on a common present-value basis. A higher discount rate reduces the present value of future returns and can materially affect whether a project appears financially attractive.
Contrast: Interest rate — an interest rate is the cost of borrowing or return on lending, while a discount rate in project evaluation is the factor used to translate future value into present value for decision making.
Example: A company compares two automation projects by discounting their expected five-year benefit streams to present value and selects the one with the stronger net present value.
Source: The Standard for Portfolio Management – Fourth Edition, section on financial value measures including present value and discounting, pp. 34-35

Core Meaning: A measurable work effort that is directly associated with completing specific project activities or deliverables.
Expansion: Discrete effort is work that can be planned, scheduled, and objectively tracked against tangible progress. In project management and earned value contexts, it contrasts with support-type effort that is not easily tied to a single definable output. It is important because performance measurement is strongest when progress can be linked to clearly scoped deliverables or tasks.
Contrast: Level of effort — discrete effort produces specific measurable outputs tied to defined activities, while level of effort supports the work without discrete end products for progress measurement.
Example: Configuring a server environment for a planned release is treated as discrete effort because completion can be measured against defined setup tasks and acceptance criteria.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The measure of work performed expressed in terms of the budget authorized for that work.
Expansion: Earned value converts completed scope into a budget-based performance measure so progress can be compared objectively with plan and spend. It is a core concept in earned value management because it links scope, schedule, and cost rather than tracking each in isolation. Teams use it to understand whether the project is truly progressing as expected, not merely spending money or reporting activity. It is especially useful where formal baselines and performance measurement are needed.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value is the budgeted value of completed work at a point in time. It answers the question of how much planned value the project has actually achieved through completed scope.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because schedule status and cost status can look acceptable when viewed separately yet still hide performance problems. Earned value is widely applied in projects that use baselines, control accounts, and formal progress measurement, especially in complex delivery environments where objective control is important.

The most common misconception: that earned value is the same as money spent or hours used. It is not a spending measure and not an effort measure; it represents the planned budget value of work that is actually complete.

How it shows up in real projects: A project control team measures completed deliverables against the performance measurement baseline, calculates EV each reporting period, and uses it with planned value and actual cost to identify variance and forecast outcomes.
Contrast: Actual cost — earned value reflects the budgeted value of completed work, while actual cost reflects what has actually been spent.
Example: A construction project budgets 100,000 of planned work for foundation completion, and when the foundation is fully complete the project records that amount as earned value even if the actual spend was higher or lower.

Core Meaning: A method of performance measurement that integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to assess project progress and forecast outcomes.
Expansion: Earned value analysis evaluates project performance by comparing planned value, earned value, and actual cost. It shows not only how much has been spent, but how much value has actually been delivered relative to the plan. PMI uses it as a structured way to monitor cost and schedule performance and support forecasting.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value analysis is the analytical use of earned value management data to determine whether project work is ahead, behind, under budget, or over budget. It relies on integrated performance measures such as planned value, earned value, actual cost, schedule variance, cost variance, and performance indices.

Why it matters and where it applies: simple spend tracking can hide the true status of a project because spending less than planned may also mean delivering less than planned. Earned value analysis is especially useful on projects with a defined baseline, measurable progress, and a need for objective forecasting, such as engineering, construction, infrastructure, defense, or large technology deployments.

The most common misconception: that earned value analysis is just a finance exercise or only useful for large projects. In reality, it is a project-control method for integrated performance insight, though it works best when scope is clearly defined and progress can be measured credibly.

How it shows up in real projects: a project controls lead compares earned value against planned value and actual cost each reporting cycle, identifies deteriorating cost performance early, and updates the forecasted estimate at completion for sponsor review.
Contrast: Earned value management — Earned value analysis is the assessment of performance using earned value data; earned value management is the broader system, processes, and governance that produce and use that data.
Example: On a construction project, the team finds that only 60 percent of planned work value has been earned while 75 percent of the budget for that period has already been spent, indicating both schedule and cost pressure.

Core Meaning: A methodology that integrates scope, schedule, and cost data to assess project performance and forecast outcomes.
Expansion: Earned value management, or EVM, compares planned value, earned value, and actual cost to show how much value has been delivered for the money spent and time elapsed. PMI uses it as a disciplined performance measurement approach that supports trend analysis, variance analysis, and forecasting rather than simple budget tracking. It is especially useful on projects that need objective progress measurement against a defined baseline.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Earned value management is a performance management method that quantifies completed work against an approved scope, schedule, and cost baseline. It converts progress into comparable measures so teams can calculate variances and performance indices.

Why it matters and where it applies: EVM gives early warning when work is slipping or costing more than planned and supports more credible forecasting at completion. It is common in large, complex, or highly governed projects such as construction, engineering, defense, and capital programs, but its logic can be scaled for smaller efforts.

The most common misconception: that EVM is just cost reporting or a finance exercise. In practice, it depends on credible scope definition, measurable progress rules, and an integrated baseline, so it is as much a planning and control discipline as a reporting method.

How it shows up in real projects: teams baseline the work, assign budgets to work packages, measure earned value as work is completed, review cost and schedule variances, and forecast estimate at completion to support corrective action.
Contrast: Variance analysis — variance analysis examines differences in performance; earned value management is the broader integrated system that generates those measures from scope, schedule, and cost baselines.
Example: On a rail-station upgrade, the team measures earned value monthly against budgeted work packages and identifies that civil works are behind schedule even though spending appears close to plan.
