Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, Second Edition — Earned Value Management, p. 13

Core Meaning: The expected total cost of completing all work expressed as the sum of actual cost to date and the forecast cost for the remaining work.
Expansion: Estimate at completion is a forecasting measure used to predict the final project cost based on current performance and assumptions about the remaining work. It helps decision makers understand whether the approved budget is still realistic or whether corrective action is needed. In earned value management, different calculation methods are used depending on whether current variances are expected to continue or are considered atypical. It is central to cost control because it turns current performance into a forward-looking view.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Estimate at completion is the projected final cost of the project or control account when all authorized work is complete. It combines what has already been spent with the best current estimate of what remains.

Why it matters and where it applies: It matters because sponsors and project managers need an early warning of likely overruns or underruns before the work is finished. It is especially relevant in projects using earned value management, periodic forecasting, and governance reviews tied to budget performance.

The most common misconception: that estimate at completion is just the original budget repeated later in the project. In practice it is a live forecast that changes when performance trends, risks, productivity, or scope conditions change.

How it shows up in real projects: During monthly performance reviews, the team recalculates EAC using actual cost, earned value indicators, and revised assumptions, then reports whether management reserve, replanning, or corrective action is required.
Contrast: Budget at completion — estimate at completion is the current forecast of final cost, while budget at completion is the original approved total budget baseline.
Example: An infrastructure project with rising labor inefficiency revises its forecast upward after three reporting periods and reports a new estimate at completion to the steering committee.
Source: PMI Lexicon — Estimate at completion; page citation not confirmed in available source

Core Meaning: The process of developing an approximation of the monetary resources needed to complete project work.
Expansion: Estimate Costs is the process used to approximate the funds required for activities, work packages, or the project as a whole. It draws on scope, schedule, resource, and risk information to produce a cost estimate. PMI treats it as a core planning activity that feeds budgeting and cost control.
Contrast: Determine Budget — Estimate Costs produces the cost estimates, while Determine Budget aggregates those estimates into an approved cost baseline.
Example: A team estimates the cost of software testing by combining labor hours, tool licences, and contingency for expected rework.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Estimate Costs, p. 241

Core Meaning: The expected cost to finish all the remaining project work.
Expansion: Estimate to complete is a forward-looking cost forecast for the work that has not yet been done. It is used with actual cost and estimate at completion to understand current cost performance and predict the final outcome. In PMI earned value and estimating contexts, it helps teams decide whether the remaining work is still likely to fit within the approved budget. It is especially useful when performance trends or scope changes suggest the original forecast may no longer be valid.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Estimate to complete is the projected cost of the unfinished portion of the project. It answers the question of how much more money will be needed to complete the remaining authorized work.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters because project leaders need a current forecast, not just a record of what has already been spent. It is used in cost control, earned value management, and periodic forecasting reviews.

The most common misconception: that ETC is the same as the original budget for the remaining work. In practice it is a live estimate that can change as productivity, risks, and scope conditions change.

How it shows up in real projects: a project team updates ETC each reporting cycle using actual performance, remaining scope, and revised assumptions, then uses it to support the estimate at completion.
Contrast: Estimate at completion — ETC is the forecast for the remaining work only, while EAC is the forecast for the total final cost.
Example: A software project has completed most of its testing but still needs defect fixes and final regression testing, so the team revises the estimate to complete for the remaining effort.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Estimate to complete (ETC)

Core Meaning: The obtaining and management of funds needed to support project work.
Expansion: Financing concerns how money is secured and made available so project activities can proceed. In project settings, it connects the business case and investment decision to the practical ability to execute the work. It is distinct from budgeting because budgeting allocates planned spending, while financing provides the money that makes that spending possible.
Contrast: Budget — financing is about obtaining and structuring the money; a budget is the planned allocation and control of that money.
Example: An infrastructure project secures bridge financing to cover design and early procurement costs before long-term capital funds are released.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Financing, p. 170

Core Meaning: An earned value technique that credits a fixed split of a work package's budget at its start and the remainder at its completion.
Expansion: The fixed formula method (such as 25/75, 50/50, or 0/100) is used to measure earned value for discrete work packages that span more than one reporting period. A set percentage of the budgeted value is earned when the work package begins, and the rest is earned only when it is finished. This avoids subjective mid-stream progress estimates and is best suited to short work packages where precise measurement is not worth the effort.
Contrast: Percent complete - percent complete credits earned value from a subjective estimate of progress, whereas the fixed formula method credits predetermined amounts only at start and finish.
Example: A team applies the 50/50 rule to a two-week documentation package, earning half the budget when work starts and the other half when the document is approved.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management - fixed formula method

Core Meaning: An estimate or prediction of future project conditions or events based on the information and knowledge available at the time.
Expansion: A forecast projects likely future performance, outcomes, or conditions using current data and assumptions. In project management it is commonly used for cost, schedule, resource, or completion expectations as work progresses. Forecasts support proactive decision making, but they remain estimates and should be updated as actual performance and risk information change.
Contrast: Estimate — a forecast predicts future outcomes based on current performance and information, while an estimate may be produced earlier to approximate effort, duration, or cost.
Example: After several reporting periods, a project manager uses earned value results to forecast the likely total cost at completion.
Source: PMI Lexicon / PMBOK glossary, Forecast; page citation not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: Estimating or predicting future project conditions or outcomes such as cost, schedule, resource needs, or performance based on current information and trends.
Expansion: Forecasting projects likely future performance by using available data, assumptions, and trend analysis to anticipate what may happen next. In project management, it commonly supports cost and schedule control by projecting estimates at completion, expected finish dates, or future resource demand. It helps teams and sponsors make timely decisions before issues become irreversible.
Contrast: Estimate — an estimate predicts a value for planned work, while forecasting updates expectations using actual performance and current conditions.
Example: A project manager uses earned value results after six months to forecast that the project will finish later than planned unless scope or staffing is adjusted.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The comparison of planned expenditure against funding limits to identify variances and adjust planned work or cash flow timing.
Expansion: Funding limit reconciliation is used to ensure planned project spending does not exceed the periodic funding available to the project. It is typically performed after cost aggregation and before the cost baseline is finalised so expenditure can be smoothed or rescheduled if needed. The result may require shifting activities, revising resource timing, or negotiating funding availability to keep the plan financially feasible.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget; funding limit reconciliation checks that baseline against actual funding constraints.
Example: A capital project shows higher planned spending in Q2 than the sponsor can release, so several procurement packages are moved into Q3 to align with the funding cap.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition and Process Groups: A Practice Guide, section on Determine Budget; term funding limit reconciliation

Core Meaning: The total and periodic amounts of funding needed to support the approved project budget.
Expansion: Funding requirements express how much money is needed and when it is needed across the life of the project. They are derived from the cost baseline and management reserves, then often presented by period or control account for governance and cash planning. They help sponsors and finance teams align project execution with available capital and release schedules.
Contrast: Project budget — the project budget is the overall authorised amount; funding requirements show the timing and distribution of that need.
Example: A transformation program needs smaller monthly funding during planning, then a larger release during system build and deployment when vendor invoices peak.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition and Process Groups: A Practice Guide, section on Determine Budget; term funding requirements

Core Meaning: The projected worth of a present sum of money at a specified future date, after earning interest or returns.
Expansion: Future value is the amount that a present sum will grow to by a given future date, assuming a rate of return, reflecting the time value of money. It is computed as FV = PV multiplied by (1 + r) raised to the power n, where r is the rate per period and n is the number of periods. In project selection, comparing the future or present value of competing investments helps identify which creates the most financial value.
Contrast: Present value - present value discounts a future amount back to today, whereas future value projects a present amount forward.
Example: A sponsor calculates that a 100,000 dollar investment earning 5 percent will have a future value of about 127,600 dollars in five years.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Costs that cannot be traced to a single project but are shared across multiple projects or operations.
Expansion: Indirect costs are expenses not directly attributable to one specific project, such as management overhead, facilities, utilities, and general administration, which are instead allocated across several projects. They contrast with direct costs, which a project incurs solely for itself. Understanding the split matters for accurate budgeting, chargebacks, and pricing.
Contrast: Direct costs - direct costs are incurred exclusively for one project, whereas indirect costs are shared overhead spread across many.
Example: The salary of a shared PMO administrator who supports ten projects is charged as an indirect cost.
Source: PMBOK Guide - indirect costs

Core Meaning: The general rise in prices over time that reduces the purchasing power of money.
Expansion: Inflation matters in project finance because future costs and contract prices may exceed today's estimates, so estimators apply escalation factors and contingency. It also affects discount rates used to evaluate long-term project economics.
Contrast: Differs from currency exchange risk, which arises from cross-border rate movements; inflation erodes value within a single currency over time.
Example: A multiyear construction estimate adds an annual escalation allowance so the budget keeps pace with rising material prices.
Source: Economics and cost estimating practice

Core Meaning: A method of calculating the discount rate at which the net present value of future cash flows becomes equal to the original investment.
Expansion: Internal rate of return is a financial evaluation measure used to assess the attractiveness of an investment or project option. PMI includes it among benefit and selection concepts used in business cases and portfolio decisions. A higher internal rate of return generally indicates a more attractive option, though it should be considered alongside strategic fit, risk, and other constraints.
Contrast: Net present value — internal rate of return expresses the break-even discount rate, while net present value expresses value as an amount at a selected discount rate.
Example: A company compares two automation projects and selects the one with the stronger internal rate of return after reviewing risk and strategic alignment.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge PMBOK Guide Sixth Edition Glossary, term Internal Rate of Return, page 721

Core Meaning: The total cost of ownership of a product, asset, or system across its useful life, not just the cost to create or acquire it.
Expansion: Life cycle cost considers the full economic impact of a deliverable from acquisition through operation, maintenance, support, and disposal. In PMI practice, it supports decisions that optimise long-term value rather than only minimising initial spend. It is especially important when alternatives have different operating, maintenance, or replacement profiles. The concept is distinct from project cost because it extends beyond the project and into the deliverable's operational life.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Life cycle cost is the cumulative cost associated with owning and using a product, asset, or system from acquisition through retirement. It treats cost as an end-to-end consideration rather than a one-time project expense.

Why it matters and where it applies: it matters when an option with a lower purchase price may be more expensive to operate, maintain, or replace over time. It commonly applies in capital projects, infrastructure, facilities, equipment selection, and product decisions where downstream costs are substantial.

The most common misconception: that life cycle cost is the same as the project budget or procurement price. In PMI terms, it is broader because it includes post-project costs incurred during use and disposition.

How it shows up in real projects: teams use it to compare alternatives such as equipment, systems, or design choices by weighing purchase cost against energy use, maintenance, reliability, and end-of-life costs.
Contrast: Project cost — life cycle cost covers the full ownership period, while project cost covers the cost of performing the project work.
Example: A hospital selects a more expensive imaging system because its lower maintenance and energy costs make it less expensive over 15 years.
Source: PMBOK Guide / PMI Lexicon — life cycle cost; exact page citation not confirmed in available PMI search results

Core Meaning: Life cycle costing is the process of considering all costs of a product, asset, or project deliverable across its full life from acquisition through operation and disposal.
Expansion: Life cycle costing extends cost thinking beyond initial project spending to include operations, maintenance, support, and end-of-life costs. PMI uses it to support better decisions when alternatives differ in total cost over time rather than only in upfront price. It is especially important when a project creates assets or capabilities that will be used for years after delivery.
Deep Dive:
What it is: life cycle costing evaluates the total cost impact of a choice across the full life of the resulting product, service, or asset. It looks past project execution cost alone and includes downstream ownership implications.

Why it matters and where it applies: many project decisions that look cheaper at the start become more expensive in operation, maintenance, or disposal. This matters in construction, infrastructure, equipment acquisition, IT platforms, and any setting where long-term operating economics influence value.

The most common misconception: that cost management ends once the project budget is approved. In practice, PMI treats good decision making as broader than initial spend, especially when selecting designs, materials, vendors, or technical approaches.

How it shows up in real projects: a team compares two HVAC systems and chooses the one with higher purchase cost but lower energy and maintenance cost over ten years, because the total life cycle cost is lower.
Contrast: Cost baseline — life cycle costing evaluates total costs across the full useful life; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased project budget for controlling project spend.
Example: A hospital construction project selects medical equipment based not only on purchase cost but also on calibration, service-contract, training, and disposal costs over the equipment's operating life.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Sixth Edition, life cycle costing, page 697

Core Meaning: Budget or time set aside for unknown-unknowns, held outside the performance measurement baseline.
Expansion: Management reserve is an amount of budget or schedule withheld for unforeseen work that is within the project's scope but cannot be specifically identified in advance, the so-called unknown-unknowns. It sits outside the cost or schedule baseline and typically requires management approval to access, after which the released amount is moved into the baseline. This distinguishes it from contingency reserve, which covers identified risks.
Contrast: Contingency reserve - contingency reserve covers identified risks and sits inside the baseline, whereas management reserve covers unidentifiable unknown-unknowns and sits outside it.
Example: Executives hold back 8 percent of the budget as management reserve for unexpected scope-related work that may emerge mid-project.
Source: PMBOK Guide - management reserve

Core Meaning: A method for estimating cost or duration by considering multiple values, typically optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic estimates, to reflect uncertainty.
Expansion: Multipoint estimating improves on a single-value estimate by explicitly incorporating uncertainty into the estimate. PMI describes it as using an average or weighted average of several estimate points to develop a more realistic expected value. It is especially useful when activity durations or costs are variable and risk needs to be reflected in planning.
Contrast: Single-point estimating — multipoint estimating reflects uncertainty through several estimate values, while single-point estimating relies on one assumed value.
Example: A scheduler estimates system testing at 8 days optimistic, 12 days most likely, and 20 days pessimistic, then uses a weighted calculation to plan the schedule.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — Multipoint estimating, page 14

Core Meaning: The future value of expected benefits expressed as the value those benefits have at the time of investment.
Expansion: Net present value, or NPV, converts expected future benefits and costs into present-value terms so investment options can be compared consistently. PMI uses it as a financial evaluation concept that considers timing as well as magnitude. It is especially useful in business cases, portfolio decisions, and project selection.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Net present value is a financial measure that expresses future cash flows in present-day terms. It accounts for the time value of money so that benefits and costs occurring at different times can be evaluated on a common basis.

Why it matters and where it applies: It supports investment decisions by helping sponsors and portfolio leaders compare alternatives with different cost profiles, benefit timing, and expected returns. It is commonly used in business case development, benefits evaluation, and project selection.

The most common misconception: that NPV is just total benefits minus total costs. In practice, the timing of those benefits and costs matters, so future amounts are discounted rather than simply added together.

How it shows up in real projects: An organisation compares two automation initiatives, one with lower upfront cost and one with larger long-term savings, and uses NPV to judge which option creates more value in present terms.
Contrast: Return on investment — NPV adjusts future cash flows to present value; ROI expresses gain relative to cost without the same time-value focus.
Example: A steering committee uses NPV to compare whether to modernise an internal platform now or defer investment in favour of a different digital initiative.
Source: The PMI Guide to Business Analysis, Net Present Value

Core Meaning: The ongoing cost required to run, support, and maintain a product, service, asset, or operational capability after or alongside project delivery.
Expansion: Operating cost refers to recurring expenditures such as staffing, utilities, maintenance, subscriptions, support, and other costs of day-to-day operation. In project management, it matters because business cases, benefits evaluations, and life cycle decisions should consider not only delivery cost but also the cost to operate the result. This makes it especially relevant in product transitions, total cost of ownership analysis, and decisions about whether a proposed solution is sustainable.
Contrast: Project cost — operating cost is the recurring cost of running the delivered result, while project cost is the temporary cost of creating it.
Example: A project to implement a new cloud platform is approved only after the team compares the implementation budget with the expected annual operating cost for hosting, support, and licensing.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The value of the benefit that is given up by choosing one option instead of the best alternative.
Expansion: Opportunity cost is the foregone value associated with selecting one project, investment, or resource use over another. In project management, it is central to portfolio selection, prioritization, business case development, and economic decision-making because choosing one initiative usually means not pursuing a competing use of funds, time, or capability. It helps decision makers compare alternatives beyond direct cost alone by considering what the organization sacrifices when it commits scarce resources.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Opportunity cost is not a cash outlay recorded in the project budget; it is the lost potential value of the next-best alternative that was not chosen. It is an economic concept used to improve decision quality when options compete for limited resources.

Why it matters and where it applies: organizations routinely choose among projects, features, vendors, schedules, and staffing assignments. Considering opportunity cost helps portfolio leaders and sponsors avoid decisions that look cheaper in isolation but destroy greater value elsewhere.

The most common misconception: that opportunity cost is the same as sunk cost or simply any project expense. Sunk cost is money already spent and irrecoverable, while opportunity cost is about unrealized future benefit from the alternative not selected.

How it shows up in real projects: a team may defer an internal efficiency project to fund a customer-facing platform with higher strategic return, or allocate a specialist to one critical initiative knowing another effort will slow as a result.
Contrast: Sunk cost — opportunity cost is the value forgone by not choosing the best alternative, while sunk cost is money already spent and not recoverable.
Example: A company funds a cybersecurity upgrade instead of a new analytics product, accepting the opportunity cost of delayed revenue growth from the product in order to reduce enterprise risk.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Fifth Edition, page 561

Core Meaning: A rough estimate made early in a project when limited information is available.
Expansion: An order of magnitude estimate is an early high-level estimate used when the scope is still emerging and uncertainty is high. Its value is directional rather than precise, helping sponsors and teams decide whether to proceed, investigate further, or compare broad options. PMI treatment of estimating recognises that estimate accuracy improves as information increases over time.
Deep Dive:
What it is: An order of magnitude estimate is a preliminary estimate produced with limited detail, often during concept development or early initiation. It gives a broad sense of expected cost, effort, or duration rather than a commitment-level figure.

Why it matters and where it applies: it supports early business cases, portfolio screening, feasibility discussions, and rough planning before detailed requirements exist. Without it, organizations may delay decisions or pretend to have precision they do not actually possess.

The most common misconception: that a rough early estimate is simply a badly prepared detailed estimate. In reality, it is a different kind of estimate matched to the amount of information available and should be communicated with appropriate uncertainty.

How it shows up in real projects: a team evaluating a new customer platform uses analogous data from prior implementations to produce a broad schedule and cost range for go or no-go decision making.
Contrast: Definitive estimate — an order of magnitude estimate is early and approximate; a definitive estimate is prepared later using much more detailed information.
Example: Before approving discovery work for a new ERP implementation, executives use an order of magnitude estimate based on similar past rollouts to judge whether the initiative is financially plausible.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Indirect costs that support project or organizational work but are not tied to a single activity.
Expansion: Overhead covers ongoing indirect costs such as facilities, administration, utilities, and management that cannot be traced directly to one deliverable. It is allocated across projects or activities and must be included when building realistic budgets and rates.
Contrast: Direct costs — direct costs are attributable to a specific activity or deliverable, while overhead is shared indirect cost spread across the work.
Example: A consultancy adds an overhead rate covering office rent and HR to each consultant's billable hour when pricing a project.
Source: Cost accounting practice — indirect and overhead costs

Core Meaning: An estimating technique that uses an algorithm based on historical data and project parameters to calculate cost or duration.
Expansion: Parametric estimating applies a statistical or mathematical relationship between a measurable parameter and the work being estimated. It is useful when reliable historical data exist and the parameter can be measured consistently, such as cost per square foot or hours per unit. PMI treats it as a formal estimating technique for developing cost or duration estimates from data-driven relationships.
Contrast: Analogous estimating — parametric estimating uses a formula tied to measurable parameters; analogous estimating relies on comparison with similar past work.
Example: A facilities project estimates interior finishing cost by multiplying the planned square footage by a validated historical cost per square foot and adjusting for current market conditions.
Source: PMBOK Guide — parametric estimating, pp. 184-185

Core Meaning: The time required for a project's cumulative benefits to recover its initial investment.
Expansion: Payback period is a financial selection metric measuring how long it takes the cash inflows from a project to repay the money invested in it. Shorter payback periods are generally preferred because the investment is recovered sooner and at lower risk. Its main weakness is that it ignores the time value of money and any benefits arising after payback is reached.
Contrast: Net present value - payback period measures only how fast the investment is recovered, whereas NPV measures the total discounted value created over the project's life.
Example: A 100,000 dollar system that saves 25,000 dollars a year has a four-year payback period.
Source: PMBOK Guide - business case / project selection methods

Core Meaning: A progress-measurement method that estimates activity or work-package completion as a stated percentage of the total authorized work.
Expansion: The percent complete method is used to assess how much work has been accomplished by expressing progress as a percentage of the whole. In PMI practice it commonly supports earned value and cost-performance reporting when objective completion evidence can be established. Its usefulness depends on having clear criteria for what counts as partial completion so reporting does not become subjective or inflated.
Contrast: 0/100 rule — percent complete allows partial credit for in-progress work; the 0/100 rule gives credit only when the work is fully finished.
Example: On a design package budgeted at 200 hours, the lead engineer determines that approved drawings and calculations represent about 60 percent of the total scope, so the package is reported as 60 percent complete for performance tracking.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: An earned value measure of progress based on the actual physical completion of work rather than time elapsed or cost spent.
Expansion: Physical percent complete estimates how much of a deliverable or work package is truly finished in observable terms. PMI uses it in earned value and progress measurement because it ties completion to tangible accomplishment rather than effort consumed. It is most useful where work can be objectively inspected, such as construction, manufacturing, installation, or equipment deployment.
Contrast: Percent complete — physical percent complete is based on observable completed work; generic percent complete is often a broader and sometimes more subjective progress estimate.
Example: If 30 of 100 network devices have been fully installed and accepted, the physical percent complete for that installation package is 30 percent.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The process of defining how project costs will be estimated, budgeted, managed, and controlled.
Expansion: Plan Cost Management is the planning process that establishes the policies, procedures, and documentation for planning, spending, and controlling project costs. Its main output, the cost management plan, sets units of measure, levels of precision, control thresholds, and reporting formats. It provides the framework that guides all later cost processes such as estimating, budgeting, and control.
Contrast: Determine Budget - Plan Cost Management sets the rules for managing cost, whereas Determine Budget aggregates estimates into the actual cost baseline.
Example: Documenting that costs will be tracked to the nearest thousand dollars with a plus or minus 10 percent control threshold.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Plan Cost Management

Core Meaning: The authorized budget assigned to scheduled work to be accomplished for an activity or work breakdown structure component.
Expansion: Planned value is the budgeted amount of work that should have been completed by a particular point in time. It is one of the core earned value measures used to compare what was planned against what was actually accomplished and spent. PMI also denotes it as PV and links it directly to the performance measurement baseline.
Contrast: Earned value — planned value is the budgeted value of scheduled work; earned value is the budgeted value of work actually completed.
Example: By the end of the second month, a data-center upgrade was scheduled to complete work budgeted at 80,000, so that amount is the project's planned value at that date.
Source: PMBOK Guide — planned value, pp. 169, 207

Core Meaning: Present value is the current worth of a future sum of money or stream of cash flows discounted at a specified rate.
Expansion: PMI uses present value in project selection, business case analysis, and financial evaluation. It helps compare options that generate costs or benefits at different times by converting future amounts into current terms. This supports decisions about whether an initiative is financially attractive relative to alternatives.
Contrast: Net present value — present value is the discounted value of a future amount or cash flow; net present value sums discounted benefits and costs to show overall financial gain or loss.
Example: A portfolio review discounts the expected savings from an automation project over three years to determine the present value of those future benefits before approving funding.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, p. 706

Core Meaning: An estimating approach that represents possible cost or duration outcomes as probabilities rather than as a single fixed value.
Expansion: Probabilistic estimating expresses uncertainty explicitly by modeling a range of possible outcomes and their likelihoods. It is used when planners need to understand confidence levels, exposure, and the spread of possible dates or costs rather than rely on one deterministic estimate. It commonly appears with three-point estimates, probability distributions, and simulation-based forecasting.
Contrast: Deterministic estimating — probabilistic estimating models a range of possible outcomes with likelihoods; deterministic estimating provides one expected value without explicitly expressing uncertainty.
Example: A schedule analyst estimates a testing phase with optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic durations to forecast the probability of meeting a launch date.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The methods used to quantify how much of a planned scope of work has been accomplished at a point in time.
Expansion: Progress measurement converts physical accomplishment into objective figures that feed earned value, forecasting, and reporting. Methods include fixed-formula rules, weighted milestones, units completed, and physical percent complete, chosen to fit the nature of each work package. Sound progress measurement minimizes subjective judgment so that schedule and cost performance reflect reality. Weak measurement, such as guessing percent complete, distorts forecasts and hides slippage.
Contrast: Effort expended — recording hours spent measures input consumed, whereas progress measurement quantifies output actually accomplished.
Example: For piping, the team measures progress by linear meters installed against the total planned, rather than by hours worked.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management (PMI)

Core Meaning: The project budget is the approved estimate for the funds needed to complete the project work.
Expansion: In PMI practice, the project budget aggregates estimated costs for project activities or work packages and establishes the authorised spending plan. It is typically time-phased to support cost control and is aligned with the cost baseline, with management reserves handled according to governance rules. The budget provides the reference point for monitoring expenditures, forecasting, and evaluating cost performance throughout the project life cycle.
Deep Dive:
What it is: The project budget is the authorised financial plan for delivering the project. It is built from cost estimates and rolled up into a level suitable for approval, funding, and control.

Why it matters and where it applies: every project needs a clear funding reference so the team can plan work, commit resources, and monitor cost performance. It is especially important in projects with procurement, phased funding, or executive oversight because spending must be tracked against an approved baseline.

The most common misconception: that the budget is just an early rough estimate. In practice, once approved it becomes a control reference, and changes to it normally require formal review and change control.

How it shows up in real projects: a project manager compares actual costs and earned value against the time-phased budget, explains variances in monthly reviews, and requests budget changes when approved scope changes increase authorised spending.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the project budget is the broader approved funding plan, while the cost baseline is the approved time-phased version used specifically to measure and monitor cost performance.
Example: A data-centre migration project receives an approved budget covering hardware, vendor labour, internal staff time, contingency, and transition support, then tracks monthly actuals against the planned spend curve.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide) Seventh Edition / Process Groups: A Practice Guide, project cost management content and funding baseline discussion; exact glossary-style page citation varies by edition

Core Meaning: The monetary resources needed to complete project work and produce the project deliverables.
Expansion: Project cost refers to the estimated and actual spending associated with carrying out project activities and acquiring required resources. In PMI practice, it is planned, budgeted, monitored, and controlled as part of project cost management. It includes direct and indirect cost elements as defined by the project's accounting and governance approach.
Contrast: Project budget — project cost is the spending associated with the work; the project budget is the approved allocation against which that spending is managed.
Example: In a warehouse automation project, project cost includes software licences, contractor labour, equipment installation, training, and testing expenses.
Source: PMBOK Guide — confirm exact citation in Infinity

Core Meaning: The knowledge area covering the processes to plan, estimate, budget, finance, fund, manage, and control costs so the project finishes within the approved budget.
Expansion: Project Cost Management is one of the PMBOK Guide knowledge areas. It groups the processes of Plan Cost Management, Estimate Costs, Determine Budget, and Control Costs. Its purpose is to complete the project within the approved budget by establishing a cost baseline and monitoring performance against it, often using earned value analysis. On many projects it is concerned primarily with the cost of the resources needed to complete activities.
Contrast: Project Procurement Management - cost management controls the project's overall budget, while procurement management governs buying goods and services from outside sellers.
Example: On a software project, the team estimates costs per work package, aggregates them into a 500K budget, and uses earned value to track spend against the cost baseline.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Project Cost Management knowledge area

Core Meaning: Forecasted expenditures for the project, by period and by funding source, that are needed to finance the project.
Expansion: Project funding requirements describe when money will be needed and from which sources it will come. They help align financing with the project schedule and expected cash flow. They can include periodic funding needs as well as approved reserves. They support budgeting, financing, and governance decisions.
Contrast: Cost baseline — the cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget for project work; project funding requirements describe the financing needed to support that spending.
Example: A capital project forecasts quarterly funding for design, procurement, and construction so finance can release funds at the right time.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Seventh Edition, project funding requirements, page 243

Core Meaning: An amount of time or budget set aside to address uncertainty and protect project objectives.
Expansion: In PMI usage, a reserve is planned capacity in time or cost that is not intended for routine work but for uncertainty. It exists because estimates are never perfectly certain and projects operate under risk. The concept includes forms such as contingency reserve and management reserve, which are used for different kinds of uncertainty and are governed differently.
Deep Dive:
What it is: A reserve is a deliberate allowance built into the plan or budget to absorb uncertainty without immediately destabilising the project. It is not extra scope and it is not a planning error; it is a controlled response to the reality of risk.

Reserves take two governed forms:

- Contingency reserve: held in the cost baseline for identified risks.
- Management reserve: held separately by senior management for unforeseen work.

Why it matters and where it applies: projects face variability in effort, cost, supply, approvals, weather, quality outcomes, and many other factors. Reserves help teams remain credible, avoid constant replanning, and respond to risk in a disciplined way across schedule, cost, and delivery commitments.

The most common misconception: that any unused reserve is waste or padding. In sound project management, reserve is justified by risk and uncertainty, governed explicitly, and released or consumed through defined controls rather than hidden inside estimates.

How it shows up in real projects: teams monitor reserve consumption as part of risk review, forecasting, and change control.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — reserve is the broader concept; contingency reserve is the portion set aside for identified risks within defined limits.
Example: A capital project includes contingency in the cost baseline for identified ground-condition risks and keeps separate management reserve for unforeseen events outside the approved baseline.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge (PMBOK Guide), Sixth Edition, reserve analysis and reserve terminology, pp. 442 and 705

Core Meaning: An analytical technique used to determine the amount of contingency and management reserves needed for the project.
Expansion: Reserve analysis evaluates whether current reserves are sufficient to address remaining uncertainty and risk. It compares reserve levels with the project s current exposure and can lead to adjustments in contingency or management reserve. PMI uses it in schedule and cost control to keep reserves aligned with actual risk conditions.
Contrast: Contingency reserve — reserve analysis is the technique used to assess reserves; contingency reserve is one type of reserve that may be evaluated.
Example: During execution, a construction team reviews weather and procurement risks and decides whether the remaining contingency reserve is still adequate.
Source: A Guide to the Project Management Body of Knowledge, PMBOK Guide, Seventh Edition — Reserve analysis

Core Meaning: A measure of how much value or benefit is gained relative to the cost of the investment.
Expansion: Return on investment is used to compare expected or actual benefits against the money and effort spent. In project management, it helps justify project selection and supports business cases and portfolio decisions. It is a financial indicator of whether a project is worth pursuing from a value perspective.
Contrast: Net present value — return on investment compares gain to cost as a ratio or percentage, while net present value converts future cash flows into a present-value amount.
Example: An organisation approves an automation project after estimating that lower operating costs and faster processing will produce a strong return on investment over three years.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The additional return demanded above the risk-free rate to compensate a party for accepting risk.
Expansion: A risk premium is the extra compensation, over and above a risk-free rate of return, that an investor or contractor expects for bearing uncertainty. In projects it appears when risk is transferred, for example a contractor pricing in a margin for accepting a fixed-price commitment. Larger or less predictable risks command larger premiums.
Contrast: Contingency reserve - a risk premium is compensation paid to a party for taking on risk, while a contingency reserve is budget the project sets aside for known risks.
Example: A contractor adds a 10 percent risk premium to a fixed-price bid to cover the chance of cost overruns it must absorb.
Source: Risk and procurement finance concept referenced in PMI guidance

Core Meaning: A very early, high-level estimate used when information is limited and uncertainty is high.
Expansion: A rough order of magnitude estimate provides a broad sense of likely cost, duration, or effort before detailed scope and requirements are known. PMI treats it as an early estimating approach that supports initial screening, feasibility discussions, and go or no-go decisions. It is intentionally imprecise and should be refined as more information becomes available.
Deep Dive:
What it is: Rough order of magnitude is an early estimate made with limited information, often during concept or initiation work. It gives decision makers a directional view rather than a commitment-level figure.

Why it matters and where it applies: it is useful for business cases, portfolio selection, and early feasibility checks when the team needs to compare options quickly. It helps avoid false precision before the project is sufficiently defined.

The most common misconception: that ROM is just a poorly prepared detailed estimate. In PMI practice, it is a different estimate class matched to the maturity of the information available.

How it shows up in real projects: a sponsor asks for a ROM to compare two potential system replacement options before authorizing detailed planning.
Contrast: Definitive estimate — rough order of magnitude is early and broad; a definitive estimate is later and based on much more detailed information.
Example: Before approving discovery work for a new CRM rollout, leadership uses a rough order of magnitude estimate to judge whether the initiative is worth pursuing.
Source: PMI Lexicon of Project Management Terms — rough order of magnitude

Core Meaning: A high-level early estimate of cost or duration with a wide accuracy range, typically -25 percent to +75 percent.
Expansion: A ROM estimate is produced early in the project, often during initiation, when little detail is known, to give stakeholders a ballpark figure for go/no-go decisions. Its accuracy range is broad, commonly cited as -25 percent to +75 percent, and it is refined into more precise estimates as the project progresses. It supports early budgeting and feasibility rather than firm commitments.
Contrast: Definitive estimate - a ROM is a wide-range early estimate, while a definitive estimate (-5 percent to +10 percent) is precise and made with detailed information.
Example: At charter time a PM gives a ROM of 500K to 1.5M for a new system before requirements are detailed.
Source: PMBOK Guide - estimate accuracy ranges

Core Meaning: A graphical representation of cumulative project data over time, often showing cost, effort, or progress.
Expansion: An S-curve shows how cumulative values typically start slowly, rise more steeply during peak activity, and then level off as the project nears completion. PMI uses it to compare planned and actual performance trends and to support forecasting. It is especially useful for communicating overall project momentum to stakeholders.
Contrast: Histogram — an S-curve shows cumulative trend over time; a histogram shows the distribution of values.
Example: A construction manager compares planned and actual cumulative labor hours on an S-curve to see whether the project is accelerating as expected.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, Second Edition — S-curve

Core Meaning: A graphic analysis technique that shows cumulative values over time, often for cost, labor hours, or progress.
Expansion: S-curve analysis uses cumulative performance data plotted over time to show how a project ramps up, accelerates, and then tapers as work completes. Practitioners use it to compare planned and actual progress patterns, identify deviations, and support forecasting.
Contrast: Earned value analysis — S-curve analysis is the visual cumulative trend display; earned value analysis is the broader performance measurement method using metrics such as EV, PV, and AC.
Example: A program manager compares planned and actual cumulative labor hours on an S-curve and sees that the actual curve is flattening earlier than expected, indicating a slowdown.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A method that expenses an asset's cost in equal amounts over its useful life.
Expansion: Straight-line depreciation spreads the depreciable cost of an asset evenly across each period of its useful life, producing a constant annual expense. It contrasts with accelerated methods that load more depreciation into the early years. Project managers encounter it in business cases and lifecycle cost analysis when justifying capital investments.
Contrast: Accelerated depreciation - accelerated methods expense more in early years, whereas straight-line spreads the cost evenly across the asset's life.
Example: A 50,000 dollar machine with a 5-year life depreciates at 10,000 dollars per year.
Source: PMBOK Guide - depreciation (cost management)

Core Meaning: A cost that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered.
Expansion: Sunk cost refers to money or effort already spent that cannot be changed by current decisions. In project management, it matters because continuation or termination decisions should be based on future costs, benefits, and strategic value rather than past expenditure. Treating sunk cost correctly helps leaders avoid escalating commitment to work that no longer makes sense.
Contrast: Opportunity cost — sunk cost is unrecoverable past spending, while opportunity cost is the value of the best alternative forgone when one option is chosen.
Example: After spending months on a prototype that no longer fits market needs, the steering group evaluates whether to stop the effort based on future return rather than the money already spent.
Source: PMBOK Guide Seventh Edition Glossary, sunk cost, page not provided in available search results

Core Meaning: The planned cost objective established for a product, component, or work effort and used as a benchmark for design, delivery, or control decisions.
Expansion: Target cost is the cost level a team aims to achieve while meeting scope, quality, and performance requirements. In project management practice, it is used to guide estimating, trade-off decisions, and cost control rather than to describe actual cost incurred. It is especially relevant where cost constraints shape design or procurement decisions.
Contrast: Cost baseline — a target cost is an objective or goal for cost performance; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased basis for measuring and controlling project cost.
Example: A product-development project sets a target cost for a new device so engineering and procurement can choose materials and features that keep the offering commercially viable.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A Lean practice where a cost target drives the design rather than the design driving the cost.
Expansion: Target value design sets an allowable cost, the target value, up front based on customer value and then steers design decisions to meet it. Cross-functional teams design to the target, treating cost as a constraint to design within rather than an outcome to be estimated afterward. It originated in Lean construction to reduce waste and deliver value within budget.
Contrast: Unlike traditional design-then-estimate, which prices a finished design and risks overruns, target value design fixes the budget first and shapes the design to fit.
Example: Given a budget for a hospital wing, the team designs each system to its allotted target cost rather than designing freely and pricing later.
Source: Lean Construction Institute; not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: The principle that a sum of money is worth more now than the same sum in the future because of its earning potential.
Expansion: Time value of money holds that money available today can be invested to earn returns, so a future cash flow must be discounted to express its present worth. This principle underpins financial appraisal techniques used to select and justify projects, including net present value, discounted cash flow, and internal rate of return. It allows costs and benefits arriving at different times to be compared on a common basis.
Contrast: Simple payback ignores the timing of cash flows, while time value of money discounts each flow according to when it occurs.
Example: An analyst discounts a project's projected five-year benefits to present value before comparing it against alternatives.
Source: PMBOK Guide - Seventh Edition

Core Meaning: A measure of the cost performance needed on the remaining work to meet a specified management target.
Expansion: To-complete performance index, or TCPI, shows the cost efficiency required from now forward rather than the efficiency already achieved. It is calculated against a target such as budget at completion or estimate at completion. Project managers use it to judge whether the remaining work must be performed at a realistic or unrealistic rate of cost efficiency.
Contrast: Cost Performance Index — TCPI shows the cost efficiency still required on remaining work, while CPI shows the cost efficiency achieved so far.
Example: After several overruns, the project manager calculates TCPI to determine whether the team can still finish within the approved budget target.
Source: PMBOK Guide — To-complete performance index, pages 206-210

Core Meaning: A financial estimate used to determine the direct and indirect costs of acquiring, operating, supporting, and eventually retiring a product or service.
Expansion: Total cost of ownership looks beyond the initial purchase price to include operating, maintenance, support, training, and disposal costs across the full life of an asset or solution. It helps project teams compare alternatives more realistically when procurement decisions have long-term consequences. In practice, it is valuable when the cheapest upfront option may create higher costs after implementation.
Contrast: Purchase price — total cost of ownership includes full life-cycle cost, while purchase price reflects only the initial acquisition cost.
Example: A project team compares two software platforms and selects the one with lower long-term support, integration, and upgrade costs even though its initial license cost is higher.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: Analyzing a product's functions to deliver them at the lowest cost without losing performance.
Expansion: Value analysis examines the required functions of a product or process and finds ways to provide those functions more economically while maintaining quality and performance. Closely tied to value engineering, it targets unnecessary cost rather than simply cutting features. It is often applied to improve an existing design or product.
Contrast: Value Engineering - value engineering typically applies this thinking during design to optimize value up front, whereas value analysis often applies it to an existing product; the terms are frequently used interchangeably.
Example: Redesigning a bracket to use less material and a cheaper process while still meeting its load requirement.
Source: PMBOK Guide - value analysis / value engineering

Core Meaning: A systematic approach to optimizing value by improving function relative to cost.
Expansion: Value engineering analyzes the functions of a project or product to achieve required performance at the lowest lifecycle cost, usually during design. It seeks to eliminate unnecessary cost while preserving or enhancing function, quality, and reliability. It is also called value analysis or value management.
Contrast: Value Analysis - value analysis usually refers to applying the same technique to an existing product, whereas value engineering is typically applied during design; the terms overlap heavily.
Example: During design, a team substitutes a standard component for a custom one, cutting cost while meeting all specifications.
Source: PMBOK Guide - value engineering

Core Meaning: A technique for determining the cause and degree of difference between the baseline and actual performance.
Expansion: Variance analysis compares planned performance with actual results to understand what changed and why. PMI notes that it can be applied to duration estimates, cost estimates, resource utilization, resource rates, technical performance, and other metrics. It is used from an integrated perspective, considering cost, time, technical, and resource variances together to understand overall project impact. The analysis supports preventive or corrective action when needed.
Contrast: Variance — variance is the deviation itself; variance analysis is the technique used to determine its cause and degree.
Example: A project is behind schedule on testing, so the team analyzes whether the delay is caused by staffing, defects, or dependency slippage before deciding on corrective action.
Source: PMI Knowledgebase — Variance Analysis

Core Meaning: A projection of the amount of budget deficit or surplus, expressed as the difference between the budget at completion and the estimate at completion.
Expansion: Variance at completion forecasts the expected difference between the approved total budget and the projected final cost. It helps the team judge whether the project is likely to finish under or over budget. PMI uses it as a cost forecasting measure within earned value management and cost control.
Contrast: Cost variance — variance at completion forecasts the final budget gap at project end; cost variance shows the current difference between earned value and actual cost.
Example: A construction project with a budget at completion of 8 million and an estimate at completion of 8.6 million shows a negative variance at completion, signalling an expected overrun.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, variance at completion, page not available in retrieved source

Core Meaning: A progress measurement technique that assigns weighted values to predefined milestones so earned value can be credited as interim accomplishments are completed.
Expansion: The weighted milestone method measures performance for longer-duration work by assigning portions of value to specific milestone completions within a work package or control account. PMI uses it in earned value management when taking credit only at full completion would hide meaningful interim progress. It improves visibility while still tying progress to objectively verifiable events.
Contrast: Percent complete method — weighted milestone method credits progress only when defined milestones are achieved, while percent complete estimates progress as a proportion of total work.
Example: On a system integration package, a team assigns 20 percent to design approval, 30 percent to build completion, 30 percent to test completion, and 20 percent to client acceptance for earned value credit.
Source: Practice Standard for Earned Value Management, weighted milestone method, page 167

Core Meaning: An earned value chart that plots cost and schedule performance over time to show trends relative to planned and earned value measures.
Expansion: A worm chart is a visual earned value display used to show how performance is trending across reporting periods. It helps teams quickly spot whether cost and schedule performance are improving or deteriorating.
Contrast: S-curve — a worm chart focuses on performance trend movement between earned value measures, while an S-curve typically shows cumulative values over time.
Example: A PMO reviews a worm chart each month to see whether a troubled infrastructure project is recovering schedule performance after corrective action.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A pricing and capacity management approach used to maximize revenue or value from limited resources by adjusting availability and allocation based on demand.
Expansion: In project contexts, yield management is an operational or business concept rather than a core PMI glossary definition. It focuses on getting the highest value from constrained capacity by shaping allocation decisions. It is most common where demand varies and available capacity cannot expand quickly.
Contrast: Resource leveling — yield management optimizes value from limited capacity; resource leveling adjusts schedules to deal with resource constraints.
Example: A consulting firm assigns its limited senior architects first to the highest-margin client initiatives during a peak delivery period.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry

Core Meaning: A budgeting method that starts from a zero base and requires each cost to be justified for the current planning period.
Expansion: Zero-based budgeting does not assume that prior budgets should roll forward unchanged. Each planned expense is reviewed and justified against current objectives, constraints, and expected value. It is used when tighter cost control or stronger alignment between spending and strategy is needed.
Contrast: Cost baseline — zero-based budgeting is a method for building or reviewing budgets from scratch; a cost baseline is the approved time-phased budget used for control.
Example: A transformation program requires each workstream to justify its staffing, software, and vendor costs for the next release cycle instead of carrying forward last quarter's allocations.
Source: not a formal PMI glossary entry
